topic: Language - Negative Impls and Auto Traits

turns.content: Imagine I have a struct called DataHolder that wraps an Rc<T> but also contains a RefCell. I want to make it !Send because the RefCell part makes it non-send, yet I need to pass the whole thing around in contexts where Send is expected. I'm stuck on how negative impls actually interact with auto traits and whether my code will ever compile if I just write impl !Send for DataHolder.

The issue here is that negative trait bounds don't work like you might think for auto traits such as Send or Sync. When you write impl !Send for MyType, the Rust compiler checks every type T where MyType implements Trait<T> and ensures it doesn't conflict with the negative bound. For DataHolder containing a RefCell, the real problem is that RefCell itself violates Send, so any container wrapping it will also be non-send by default unless you explicitly opt out using PhantomData. The key insight is that negative impls only affect types that implement Trait<T> for some T; they do not force all types to respect !Send automatically without considering the actual dependencies in your struct.

So if I just write impl !Sync and impl !Send for DataHolder, will that actually prevent the compiler from checking whether DataHolder contains something Send? It feels like I'm just adding noise to my code when I really need to fix the underlying type issue. Is there a simpler way to handle this without negative trait bounds?

Negative impls are essential for soundness because they allow you to express that certain types cannot be shared across threads or synchronized, but they don't magically make your entire struct compliant with Send or Sync if it contains incompatible types. The real solution when wrapping a RefCell is using PhantomData<T> as a marker type in your struct instead of actually storing the problematic value directly. By declaring impl !Send for DataHolder and then adding a PhantomData<RefCell> field that you don't actually use, you can signal to the compiler that this specific type should never be Send or Sync without breaking other parts of your codebase.

That sounds promising but I'm worried about accidentally using PhantomData in production. How exactly do I combine negative impls with auto trait propagation rules so that my DataHolder remains non-send even if someone tries to use it in a context expecting Send? Is there a way to make the compiler stop complaining when I pass DataHolder to a function that requires Send?

When you define impl !Send for DataHolder and include PhantomData<RefCell> in your struct, the auto trait propagation rules ensure that any type T where DataHolder implements Trait<T> will inherit this non-send status. The compiler won't allow you to pass DataHolder to a function requiring Send because the negative bound effectively blocks the automatic inference of Send for your type. If you need to work with such types in multi-threaded code, you must explicitly opt out using PhantomData and avoid passing these types across thread boundaries unless you've carefully analyzed their internal composition.

I still think I'm misunderstanding something fundamental here. Can you clarify how negative impls interact with the auto traits like Send so that when I write impl !Send for MyType, it actually prevents any type T from being considered Send if MyType implements Trait<T>? It seems too restrictive and I fear my code will just break everywhere.

Negative trait bounds are sound because they constrain how types can be composed in the type system. When you write impl !Send for DataHolder, you're telling the compiler that this specific type cannot participate in Send-related operations regardless of its internal fields. The auto traits like Send and Sync propagate through trait implementations but negative bounds override this propagation by explicitly stating that certain combinations are forbidden. Your code won't break everywhere; instead, it will correctly reject any attempt to use DataHolder in a Send context while allowing you to safely use it in single-threaded scenarios or with PhantomData markers.

Okay I think I get the basic idea but I'm still confused about how this affects my actual Rust code. If I have a function that takes an argument of type MyType and requires that argument to be Send, will negative impls prevent me from calling this function? And what happens if I want to use PhantomData instead of actually storing the problematic value?

Yes, negative impls will prevent you from passing DataHolder to a function requiring Send because the compiler checks every type that implements Trait<T> against your !Send declaration. If you replace the actual RefCell with PhantomData<RefCell>, the struct becomes smaller and safer for single-threaded use cases while still maintaining the !Send guarantee through auto trait propagation. The key is understanding that PhantomData acts as a lightweight marker that preserves the negative bound without adding runtime overhead, allowing you to express intent clearly without breaking your type system.

topic: Language - Negative Impls and Auto Traits

turns.content: Hey, I'm onboarding to this new codebase and I'm confused about negative impls. If I add `impl !Send` for a type, does it mean that type is never safe to send across threads? Is it just like saying the type doesn't implement Send?

That's a great start but you're missing a key detail. `impl !Send` means the compiler assumes your type cannot be safely sent between threads, which triggers soundness checks to reject any code that tries to use it across thread boundaries. It doesn't just mean 'not Send', it actively prevents unsafe operations by design.
Oh okay, so if I have a struct with an `Rc<T>` field and I want to make sure the whole thing is not sendable, do I just add `impl !Send` on top of it?

Almost right. If you manually implement `impl !Send`, that's fine, but if your type contains an `Rc<T>` or other non-unsafe types, the compiler will still see those as Send and Sync automatically due to auto trait propagation rules. The negative impl doesn't override what the fields already imply.
Wait, so even if I write `impl !Send` on a type that has an `Rc<T>` inside, it won't actually prevent sending because the auto trait rules will still allow it?

Exactly. That's why you need to use `PhantomData` to opt out of auto traits if you want negative impls to take effect. If your type has an `Rc<T>` and you want it truly unsendable, you must replace the `Rc<T>` with something like a `PhantomData<UnsafeCell<T>>`, then add `impl !Send` on top.
Ah, I see. So if I use `PhantomData<UnsafeCell<T>>` and then add `impl !Sync`, that means the type can't be sent across threads at all?
Yes, exactly. With `PhantomData<UnsafeCell<T>>` and both negative impls set up correctly, your type becomes truly unsendable because it's no longer subject to auto trait propagation rules anymore.

topic: Language - Negative Impls and Auto Traits

turns.content: I'm working on a personal side project this weekend and I keep hearing about negative trait implementations, but I don't get how they actually work or why the compiler enforces them so strictly.

Negative traits are enforced by soundness because Rust's type system guarantees that if a type implements a positive trait, it can be safely shared across threads. When you write impl !Send for MyType, the compiler uses soundness rules to ensure no negative cycles exist in the dependency graph, so unsafe code cannot slip through.
Okay, I thought if I just declared a type and wrote impl !Send for it, that would block all access automatically without any other checks.

That's not quite right. While you can write impl !Send for MyType, the compiler only checks this at compile time based on the type hierarchy and trait bounds. If another part of your code incorrectly assumes Send without checking the actual negative implementation, that will cause a hard error later when linking or running.
I see. So I can't just rely on the negative impl alone to prevent unsafe access at runtime?
Exactly. The negative implementation is a compile-time guarantee, but you must also ensure no positive Send implementations are derived for that type through inheritance or composition.
That makes sense. But what about auto traits like Unpin? Do they follow the same negative impl rules?

Auto traits have special propagation rules that differ from regular traits. They automatically propagate to supertraits and implement themselves for all types unless you explicitly disable it, which is why auto traits like Unpin are so common in the standard library.
I thought I could just use PhantomData to opt out of these automatic implementations if needed. Is that true?

PhantomData helps with positive trait bounds by proving a type is empty or has certain properties, but for negative traits you still need to manually write impl !Trait because auto traits don't support negative implementations in the same way.
So I have to explicitly block Send and Sync if my type shouldn't be shared?
Yes, you must write impl !Send for MyType; impl !Sync; manually. This tells the compiler your type cannot be safely transferred between threads or moved across process boundaries.
Got it. One last question: if I use a wrapper struct that contains only PhantomData, can I still make it Send and Sync?
If the wrapper struct only holds PhantomData instances, then yes you can implement both Send and Sync for it because PhantomData itself has no data dependencies that would violate safety.

topic: Language - Negative Impls and Auto Traits

turns.content: I just tried to write a custom wrapper around some thread-safe data, but I got an error saying my type doesn't implement Send. It feels like Rust is refusing to let me put this in a shared memory block even though the underlying struct seems fine. Is it similar to how C++ template metaprogramming might reject unsafe types before runtime?

That's actually exactly what happens here, but instead of a compile-time rejection in C++, Rust enforces this at the type level using negative trait bounds. The error you're seeing comes from the compiler checking if your impl satisfies all the requirements for Send and Sync. If any field inside your struct doesn't implement these traits, the whole thing fails because negative impls are how Rust proves that a type can never be shared across threads.

So I tried adding PhantomData to my struct like in some examples online but it didn't work. The error still says Send isn't implemented even though the actual data part is fine. Why does PhantomData fail here?

PhantomData doesn't help because it only implements positive traits like Send and Sync, not negative ones. When you need to opt out of these, you can't rely on PhantomData since it propagates auto traits in the opposite direction. You actually want to use a type that explicitly implements !Send or !Sync, which is how you tell Rust your type intentionally cannot be shared.
Wait, so I should just write impl !Send for MyType;? But then why does the compiler complain about auto trait propagation if I'm trying to implement negative bounds?

The issue is that auto traits like Send and Sync propagate automatically through composition unless you explicitly break them. If your struct contains a field that implements !Send, Rust will still try to apply the positive trait rules based on how auto traits are inherited unless you manually declare impl !Sync for your entire type structure.
Okay so I need to write both impl !Send and impl !Sync? But what if one of my fields already has a negative impl, does that automatically make the parent struct safe?

Not quite. Auto traits don't propagate negatively by default in older Rust versions unless you explicitly implement them. If your field implements !Send, it won't automatically give your parent type !Send unless you declare impl !Send for the parent struct itself. You have to be explicit about which negative bounds apply to your entire type.

This is getting confusing because I thought auto traits would just follow everything down like positive ones do. Can you walk me through a concrete example where I declare impl !Sync for a struct containing an Option<i32>?

Sure, here's the pattern: You define your struct with fields that might contain unsafe data, then add impl !Send and impl !Sync to explicitly mark it as non-shareable. For an Option<i32>, you need to declare impl !Sync because even though i32 is safe, the container itself must opt out of positive propagation rules.
So if I have a struct with both String and Mutex<i32> inside, do I still need to write impl !Sync? Or does the Mutex handle it?

The Mutex<i32> already implements !Send because it can't be moved out of a thread-safe context without locking. However, your wrapper struct still needs to explicitly declare impl !Sync and impl !Send so that the negative bounds propagate correctly through auto trait rules.
I think I finally get it now. One last question though—what happens if someone tries to use this type in a context where Send is expected?

Rust will refuse to allow the operation because the negative impl prevents any code from treating your type as Send or Sync. The compiler won't generate invalid borrow chains, so you'll get a clear error message explaining that the type doesn't satisfy the trait requirements.

topic: Language - Negative Impls and Auto Traits

turns.content: Hey, I'm stuck on my CI/CD pipeline because my new library is failing the build. It seems like the compiler is complaining about some trait bounds that don't make sense to me right now. Specifically, it looks like there's a type T where we can't guarantee Send or Sync safety without using PhantomData tricks. Can you help me understand why this happens?

Hey! That sounds frustrating. The issue is likely related to how Rust handles negative trait implementations and auto traits like Send and Sync. When a type doesn't implement one of these, it can block other types from being sent or synced with that value unless you explicitly handle the propagation rules.

So basically, if I have a struct that holds some internal state but doesn't implement Send, then any wrapper around it also can't be Send? That seems too strict for my use case where I'm just using PhantomData to mark the data as unsafe.

Exactly. The compiler enforces soundness by ensuring that if a type cannot implement Send, then no other type can claim it implements Send either unless you explicitly opt out using negative trait bounds or PhantomData strategies to mark the data as unsafe.
Wait, so I could just write impl !Send for MyType; and that would automatically prevent other types from being Send? But what about auto traits like Sync?

Yes! Negative trait implementations are a powerful feature in Rust. When you use impl !Send for MyType, the compiler knows that MyType cannot be sent across threads. This prevents other types from claiming Send unless they explicitly handle this constraint.
But I'm still getting errors about auto trait propagation rules. It seems like even if I don't implement Sync, the compiler is complaining because of some hidden rule somewhere?

Right. Auto traits like Send and Sync have special propagation rules in Rust. If a type doesn't explicitly opt out using negative trait bounds or PhantomData tricks to mark it as unsafe, the compiler assumes it must implement these traits if any of its fields do.
So maybe I need to use PhantomData to manually declare that this data is intentionally not safe? Like impl !Send for MyType; but only after using PhantomData?

Not quite. You don't necessarily need PhantomData unless you want to explicitly mark a field as unsafe. Instead, you can use negative trait bounds directly on the type itself without extra markers if your goal is just to prevent Send or Sync.
Okay, so I should just add impl !Send and impl !Sync to my struct? And then maybe use PhantomData only when I actually need to hide a field?

That's mostly correct. Negative trait bounds are the primary way to opt out of Send or Sync in Rust. You can also combine them with PhantomData if you want to explicitly mark a specific field as unsafe.
But what if I have multiple fields, some safe and some unsafe? How do I handle that case?

In that scenario, you would use PhantomData for the unsafe fields to explicitly mark them as not implementing Send or Sync. This allows you to keep the safe fields intact while preventing the entire type from being sent across threads.
Got it. So I can mix and match positive and negative trait implementations depending on my needs? And this is why my CI/CD pipeline was failing?

Yes! The CI/CD failure likely occurred because your type didn't properly handle the Send or Sync constraints. By adding impl !Send and impl !Sync where needed, you can satisfy the compiler's soundness checks without breaking other parts of your codebase.
One more thing though—what happens if someone tries to send this type across threads despite the negative impls? Will Rust just silently allow it?
No, Rust will enforce soundness at compile time. If you try to pass a type with negative trait bounds across threads without proper handling, the compiler will reject it immediately before runtime.
That makes sense! So using negative impls is basically a safety net for my pipeline? And I should avoid relying on PhantomData unless absolutely necessary?

Exactly. Negative trait bounds are your best friend here. Use them to clearly express that certain data is intentionally unsafe, and only reach for PhantomData when you need to selectively hide fields.

topic: Language - Negative Impls and Auto Traits

turns.content: I've been reading about negative trait bounds in Rust for a while now, but I still don't fully grasp why they are necessary for soundness.

That is a great question. Think of it like this: when you define an `impl !Send` or `impl !Sync`, you are explicitly telling the compiler that your type cannot be safely shared across threads because it might contain mutable state or internal pointers that violate the safety guarantees. Without these negative bounds, the compiler assumes everything is safe by default unless proven otherwise.
So I thought if I just marked my struct with `#[derive(Debug)]` it would automatically prevent sending to threads? Does that work?

Not at all. Deriving Debug does not affect thread safety or the ability to send data between threads. The compiler only checks if your type implements `Send` and `Sync`. If you want a type to be unsendable, you must explicitly add negative trait bounds like `impl !Send for MyType; impl !Sync for MyType;` in your code.
Ah okay. But what happens if I have a struct that contains an Option<T> where T is Send, but the Option itself isn't Send? Does Rust automatically propagate the negative bounds to the inner type?

That is exactly why auto traits are tricky. Negative trait bounds like `!Send` and `!Sync` are not auto traits, so they do not propagate automatically through generics or field composition. If you have a struct containing an unsendable type, the outer struct will also fail to implement Send unless you explicitly add the negative bound to it.
Wait, so if I use `PhantomData` with a negative bound, does that help? Like `impl !Send for MyType where PhantomData<Unsized> implements !Send`?

You are very close but need to be careful. You cannot use `PhantomData` with negative bounds directly because `PhantomData` only supports positive trait bounds for safety checking purposes. To opt out of Send or Sync, you must manually define the type and add explicit negative bounds on the struct itself.
Can I use `PhantomData` with a negative bound to suppress warnings? Like `impl !Send for MyType where PhantomData<Unsized> implements !Send`?

No, that won't work. Since negative bounds are not auto traits, you cannot use them as generic parameters in the same way you would with positive ones. You must simply write `impl !Send for MyType;` directly without relying on PhantomData.
Okay, so I understand now that negative bounds are explicit and not automatic. But what about auto traits like `Sized`? Can they be used to enforce these constraints?

Auto traits like `Sized` behave differently because they propagate automatically through generics, but negative bounds do not. If you want your type to be unsized or unsendable, you must explicitly write the negative bound in your impl block rather than relying on automatic propagation.
Got it. So if I have a generic function that returns a type with `impl !Send`, will the return type automatically be unsendable?

Yes, but only if you explicitly mark the return type or use negative bounds on the struct itself. The compiler does not auto-propagate unsendability unless you add explicit constraints to your function signature.
